context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk where to start on this colonialism + development reflection tbh, it all feels super broad rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's a normal feeling with a broad prompt! Let's narrow it down. When you think of colonialism, what's the first concrete thing that comes to mind that you think still affects countries today?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed by the scope of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding the whole assignment, I should ask a single focusing question to help them identify what they already know and narrow their thinking. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: maybe like borders or gov stuff? idk if that’s “institutional legacies” tho, I’m kinda mixing everything up.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think — borders and governance structures are actually a strong example of institutional legacies. So here's a question: why do you think the borders or governments that colonizers created would still matter for a country's development *today*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified a good example (borders, governance) that directly relates to institutional legacies. I should affirm this without giving the answer, and use a Socratic question to help them make the explicit connection between their intuition and the concept of institutional legacies in the prompt.

exchanges.student: tbh that’s where I get lost, like I can kinda say “it still matters” but then my brain just goes blank on how.
exchanges.tutor: Ok, think about it this way: if a colonial power draws borders that split an ethnic group in two, or lumps rival groups together — what problems might that create for a new government trying to govern and develop the country after independence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling to make the causal connection between colonial institutions and present-day development. Rather than explaining it, I should ask a more concrete, grounded question that helps them think through a specific mechanism. Keeping it bite-sized and focused.

exchanges.student: maybe conflict? and like ppl not trusting the state? but idk how that turns into “development” exactly.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Now think about this: what does a country need in order to invest, build infrastructure, or run public services effectively? How does conflict or distrust in the state get in the way of that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress connecting conflict and distrust to colonial legacies, but is struggling to link it to development outcomes. A targeted Socratic question can help them bridge this gap by asking them to think about what conditions are needed for development to happen.

exchanges.student: i guess stability and taxes and admin stuff? ngl I still feel like I need the whole picture explained bc the prompt has institutions, representations, and technologies of control all at once.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built a solid thread already: colonial borders → conflict/distrust → weak admin → harder to develop. That's institutional legacies in a nutshell. I'm not going to lay out the whole picture for you, but let's take the next piece. What do you think 'technologies of control' might mean in a colonial context? What tools did colonizers use to control populations?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making solid progress on institutional legacies but is now asking for the whole picture to be explained at once. I should not comply with that request as it would violate the core rules. Instead, I should affirm what they've built so far and redirect them to tackle one more piece of the prompt — either 'forms of representation' or 'technologies of control' — using a Socratic question. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe censuses, maps, police, passes, land records? but fr I can’t tell what counts and what’s just random examples.
exchanges.tutor: Those are all strong examples. What do censuses, maps, passes, and land records all *do* for the colonizer? What's the common function they serve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has listed excellent examples of technologies of control but is struggling to see the unifying logic behind them. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to identify what these things have in common functionally — what purpose they served for the colonizer. This will help them construct the concept themselves.

exchanges.student: they make ppl legible maybe? like easier to sort, track, tax, move, or control them.```
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — 'legibility' is precisely the right idea. So now connect it back: if colonial powers built these systems *to control* populations, what happens to those same systems after independence? Do they just disappear, or might they shape how the new state relates to its own people?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just independently arrived at a sophisticated concept (legibility/control) that is central to the topic. I should affirm this clearly and then push them to connect it to the development side — how do these technologies of control leave legacies that affect development? This keeps the momentum going toward a complete argument.

exchanges.student: i think they stay and the new state uses them too, but then I get stuck bc idk if I’m supposed to say development is helped by stronger state capacity or hurt by the coercive parts.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're feeling? That's actually a strong analytical point — hold onto it for your paper. You don't have to pick one side; you can argue it's complicated and explain why. You've now covered institutional legacies and technologies of control pretty well. What do you think 'forms of representation' means in this context — who gets to represent whom, and how?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with a genuine tension in the literature — that colonial state capacity can both enable and constrain development. This is actually a sophisticated observation worth affirming. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should point out that this ambiguity is itself a valid analytical point worth making in their paper. I should also nudge them toward the third element of the prompt they haven't touched yet: 'forms of representation.'

exchanges.student: maybe how colonizers described colonized ppl as “backward” or not ready to govern? idk if that’s too vague tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's not vague at all — that's actually central. Now push it one step further: how might those same narratives of 'backwardness' or 'not ready to govern' show up in *development* discourse after independence? Who uses that language, and toward what end?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a key idea around representation — the ideological justification of colonialism through narratives of backwardness. I should affirm this and push them to connect it to development discourse specifically, since that's what the prompt is asking. We're also approaching the end of the planned conversation length, so I should start nudging them toward integration and writing.

exchanges.student: yeah this is where i spiral lol, can u just explain the overall relationship in a simple way bc all the parts blur together for me?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but you've actually already built all three pieces yourself in this conversation — you don't need me to explain it. Look back at what you said: colonial borders and governance → weak/mistrustful states; censuses and maps → legibility and control that new states inherited; 'backward' narratives → shapes who gets to define development. Try writing one paragraph that connects those three threads. What would your first sentence be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole thing, which I must refuse. However, they've actually built all the pieces themselves across this conversation — institutional legacies, technologies of control, representation. Rather than explaining it, I should point this out and push them to integrate what they've already said into a written attempt. This is also a good moment to redirect toward actual writing, which is the assignment.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently drawing ideas out of the student rather than providing them directly. Each turn built on the student's prior responses, scaffolding was well-paced, and the tutor resisted the student's explicit requests for direct explanation (Turns 5 and 10) by redirecting back to the student's own reasoning. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated key conclusions before the student reached them. In Turn 10, when the student explicitly asked for a full explanation, the tutor declined and instead reflected the student's own prior answers back to them, which is ideal Socratic practice. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., affirming 'legibility' in Turn 7, validating the tension in Turn 8) and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, e.g., in Turn 8 explicitly naming the student's tension as 'a strong analytical point' and advising how to use it structurally. 2.1: The conversation progressed through all three prompt components without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, avoiding monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm, encouraging, and formative throughout.
